trajectory_summary: The call never left the starting gate. Mustafa opened with a multi-part hostile objection — disputed request, financial constraint, credit anxiety, explicit 'leave me alone' — and the rep had no structured response to any of it. The rep's only substantive move was a features explanation that addressed none of Mustafa's stated concerns, followed by a binary credit question that produced a hard 'no' and ended the call. Energy did not build; it leaked from the first exchange and was gone by 01:44.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is 'unknown.' Based on the transcript, the correct tag is 'DQ / no appointment' — the rep accepted a self-reported credit disqualification and offered only a vague callback with no locked time. No appointment was booked or tentatively set.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Hostile-open objection handling — acknowledge before redirecting
primary_coaching_focus.quote: My credit, I don't have no good credit to get financed solar. You gotta leave me alone.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The rep's entire call arc was determined in the first 40 seconds: Mustafa disclosed financial vulnerability and explicit hostility, and the rep had no structured response. Every subsequent move (features explanation, binary credit question, vague callback) was downstream of this initial failure to acknowledge. This is the rep's highest-leverage coaching gap on this call — not a technique gap but a foundational 'what do I do when someone says leave me alone' gap. The manager should use this moment to walk through the acknowledge-then-bridge structure before any redirect attempt.

key_moments.quote: My credit, I don't have no good credit to get financed solar. You gotta leave me alone.
key_moments.stewart_read: Mustafa is disclosing financial vulnerability and frustration simultaneously — 'leave me alone' is not a negotiating position, it is a person who feels cornered. The rep's response ('Gotcha. Gotcha. So,') acknowledges nothing and bridges to nothing. The codex classifies this as a trust gift: the customer is telling the rep exactly what is blocking him. The correct move is to acknowledge the specific concern before attempting any redirect.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. I mean, the only free part, Mustafa, is we take care of the installation fee and no down payment, no upfront cost. So the only thing that you're paying for is just, you know, a cheaper solar power payment because we eliminate your current electric bill.
key_moments.stewart_read: Mustafa's objection is 'it's not free' — a cost/financial frame. The codex's objection_inversion move is directly applicable: the flip available here is to redirect the cost frame toward the ongoing expense of the current electric bill rather than defending the product's cost structure. Instead the rep explains features (installation fee, no down payment) — a defensive move that stays on the customer's terms rather than inverting them. This is the universal coaching gap the codex flags across the corpus: bill capture and cost objections handled safely instead of flipped.

key_moments.quote: But what's even but you're gonna post some on this. They they didn't run credit to this one. It's just two months.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the strongest evidentiary anchor for a prior-contact hypothesis: Mustafa references 'they didn't run credit to this one' and 'just two months,' suggesting a prior Ion (or competitor) touchpoint within the last two months where a credit pull was discussed but not executed. If this is a recycled lead, the rep is treating it as a cold inbound — missing context that could have reframed the conversation ('I see you spoke with someone a couple months ago — I wanted to follow up on that'). The rep does not engage with this disclosure at all.

The rep asks a binary threshold question — 'is your credit at 650?' — after Mustafa has already stated three times that his credit is insufficient. This converts a stated concern into a hard-DQ gate. The codex's spencer_operator_note flags exactly this pattern: stated credit is unreliable (customers see Vantage scores, not FICO; bureaus differ by 30-50 points), and the open policy question is whether the setter should DQ on stated credit at all or pass to the closer's actual pull. By asking the binary question and accepting 'No' as a terminal answer, the rep may have killed a deal that the closer's credit pull would have qualified. Additionally, the threshold stated is 650, not the schema's 670 — a threshold that may differ from the schema's documented figure, an open policy question pending Kenny's resolution.
